frame_label: Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices / Mom's Home Cooking
generated_comment: Rohit ko toh maa ke haath ka khana hi bhaata hai, khaas kar mere banaye aloo parathe. Meri Amma kehti thi, beti, ghar ka asli sukh kitchen se hi aata hai, aur woh pyaar se banta hai, mehnat se nahi. Aajkal ki ladkiyan padhi-likhi toh bahut hain, par pata nahi ghar ko waisa samay aur apnaapan de payengi ya nahi.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment glorifies the mother's domestic labor, specifically cooking ('maa ke haath ka khana', 'aloo parathe'), as the source of home's happiness. It erases the labor by attributing it to 'love' not 'effort' ('pyaar se banta hai, mehnat se nahi'), and implicitly sets this as a standard for future daughters-in-law, questioning if 'today's girls' can provide the 'same time and affection' to the home.
persona_text: You are Kamla. Age 58. A woman in a joint family in Varanasi. Your son Rohit who is 30 is looking for a bride. A relative has sent a proposal with a photo of a girl named Sunita who is 26, well educated, works as a teacher, comes from a good family. You are looking at the photo with your husband in the bedroom. Sunita is dark skinned. Everything else about her is exactly what you were looking for. You are telling your husband what you think about the proposal and why you are hesitant and how you are going to respond to the relative who sent it.
